name: Richat Structure
description: The Richat Structure, also known as the Eye of the Sahara, is a prominent circular feature in the Adrar Plateau of Mauritania. It has captured the attention of researchers and enthusiasts alike, leading to various interpretations regarding its origin and potential significance. Mainstream geology attributes the Richat Structure to a highly symmetrical and deeply eroded geological dome. This explanation posits that the rings are composed of different types of sedimentary and igneous rocks that have been uplifted and subsequently eroded over millions of years. The varying resistance of these rock layers to erosion has resulted in the concentric ring appearance. Alternative theories, however, propose more speculative origins. Some connect it to Atlantis, suggesting the structure is the remains of the lost city described by Plato. Proponents cite the structure's circular shape, concentric rings, and location as aligning with Plato's description. Other theories propose extraterrestrial involvement, suggesting the structure is the result of a meteor impact or even an ancient alien construction. These theories often point to the structure's unusual symmetry and size as evidence against natural geological processes. The mainstream scientific community largely dismisses these alternative theories, citing a lack of concrete evidence and the well-established geological explanation. Geological surveys, rock dating, and erosion analysis support the dome formation theory. The debate continues, fueled by the structure's visual intrigue and the desire to uncover hidden histories.
subtitle: The Eye of the Sahara's Enigmatic Origin

claims.claim: The Richat Structure is the lost city of Atlantis.
claims.evidence: Circular shape, concentric rings, location matching Plato's descriptions.
claims.criticism: Plato's description is vague, and the geological explanation is far more plausible based on evidence.

claims.claim: The Richat Structure is the result of a meteor impact.
claims.criticism: Lack of evidence of impact crater features like shocked quartz or impact breccia. The dome structure explains the formation.

claims.claim: The Richat Structure is an ancient alien construction.
claims.criticism: No scientific evidence of alien involvement. Natural geological processes can explain the structure's features.

keyFigures.name: Plato
keyFigures.role: His writings on Atlantis are used to connect the Richat Structure to the lost city.

keyFigures.name: G. Théodore Monod
keyFigures.role: Early geologist who studied the Richat Structure.

keyFigures.name: Scientists studying impact craters
keyFigures.role: Their research into impact formations helps to rule out meteor impact as the cause.

mythologyConnections: Possible connection to local Mauritanian folklore regarding unusual geological formations

timeline.event: First aerial photographs reveal the Richat Structure.
Geological studies investigate the structure's origin.
Alternative theories emerge, linking the structure to Atlantis and extraterrestrial activity.

sources.author: Thomas G. Farr, Ronald Blom
sources.work: Richat Structure, Mauritania: Interpreted as an Inverted Impact Crater
sources.title: Thomas G. Farr, Ronald Blom

sources.author: Guillaume Matton, Michel Jébrak, Ahmedou Ould Hamed
sources.work: The Richat Structure, Mauritania: An Endogenous Geodynamic Structure
sources.title: Guillaume Matton, Michel Jébrak, Ahmedou Ould Hamed
